sentences.text: Paula Flancbaum.
F as in Frank, L-A-N-C-B-A-U-M.
My name was Lerman, L-E-R-M-A-N.
March the 12th, 1920.
I was born in Bialobrzegi, Poland.
B-I-A-L-O-B-R-Z-E-G-I
Not far from Warsaw.
My mother's name was Sarah Tema Koper.
My father's name was Luzer Lerman.
I have living one brother.
We were six children-- four girls and two boys.
And they all perished.
They were killed.
Yes.
My oldest was Esther Rifka.
The second one was Schindel.
The third was a boy.
He was Meyer Nucen.
The fourth was a boy, Isser.
Later came a girl, Bridal Yitta.
And I was the youngest, Paula.
My father was a bookkeeper.
And he was an expert on forest.
When he went in a forest, he could tell how much money they would get, how much lumber.
He would come out for burning or for buildings.
No, she was a housewife.
We lived-- we rented.
We weren't very rich, but we were comfortable.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My oldest sister went to Radom, a town not far from us, to learn.
My father said that the kids are growing up.
He wrote a letter to his family.
It's a small town, he said.
And I have four girls.
And we can't go to high school or college.
He must learn-- one must learn a trade.
So she went.
She stayed with my uncle for a year, and she learned a trade.
And she came back, and she was a seamstress.
And all the sisters learned from her.
Well, I was a tomboy-- youngest.
I played with boys only.
I played with money, throwing coins on the wall.
I worked-- I played with other things.
I climbed trees.
Once, I even fell in-- it's called a [NON-ENGLISH].
That means where the filth comes out from the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] toilet.
This was the day when my sister came with my mother back from Radom.
And I was-- my aunt took me in, and she gave me a bath from head to toe in a barrel.
We didn't have showers.
And that-- that was my life.
Yes.
I went to school, but we only had seven grades.
But I think the seven grades was, like, almost, here high school.
Yes.
We were orthodox, but my father was, like, orthodox, but he wanted the best for the kids.
My sister wanted to marry a boy, but he went to Israel in 1938 or '37.
And my mother said, not in her life.
She wouldn't send away a child.
And my father said, I want to hear good news from far away.
But nothing came out of it.
And she's now dead, was killed.
She lived in Warsaw.
She married a nice fellow, and they lived comfortable.
And they had one child.
And when the war started, she came.
We took-- I was there for the last few months, because the first, when she was pregnant, she lost two children-- twins.
So by the second, the doctor said she should take care of herself.
She should stay in bed the last few months.
So my father sent me to Warsaw, and I stayed over the 1939 bombs in Warsaw.
And I tried to help her as much as I could.
I stayed in lines for bread, for milk, to bring her something to eat.
What was what like?
Oh, Shabbat.
Well, everybody had to be Friday night by the table.
Once I had Gentile friends, too.
I was a young girl, and we played.
I played with the Gentile girls in the street.
We covered our eyes, and I should guess where I am.
And it was Friday night, and I forgot that my father comes from shul to make Kiddush.
So they were looking for me all over.
Finally they find me.
I had covered my eyes with a cloth.
And they found me in this street.
This is the first time in my life my father spanked me in the face.
Since then, I never missed a Shabbat [INAUDIBLE].
Right.
Yes.
Just one grandfather I remember.
And I know I remember all of them.
Should I say the names?
A few.
Just a few.
I had [NON-ENGLISH] and Uncle [?
Suel ?].
And they had [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] four-- five children, two boys and three girls.
And they all perished.
One boy was working for the SS.
And he was traveling on the road, on a truck.
And in the back of him were riding SS people-- soldiers.
And they was giving him signal to get off the road, and he didn't have where to go.
So they stopped him, and they killed him-- two boys.
Well, it was a nice community.
But through the end was-- Poles-- anti-Semitism was a lot.
But I wanted to tell you this story.
When we took home our sister from Warsaw with a little baby, she was a year and a half [INAUDIBLE] that time was before Pesach.
And my brother was traveling to Radom.
It wasn't a ghetto yet.
And he was in a truck with a lot of people.
And the guy, the owner, from the truck stopped in the middle, and he asked for money.
Everybody should pay him.
One gentile girl didn't want to pay.
So he opened the door, and she should go down if she doesn't want to pay.
She came home.
She went to the SS, and she said that he raped her.
So he asked my brother if he could be a witness, how it was.
So he said, yes.
I'll go with you to the police station.
So she saw him.
She said, he raped me too, for my brother.
And that time was very, very bad.
They sent-- sent my brother to Radom in jail.
This was the hardest time.
When my father wanted to make Kiddush, he couldn't.
He stopped.
And we all started to cry.
He wasn't long there.
We tried, because my sister-in-law's father was the president of our town.
And we had an uncle in Radom, also.
And he was two weeks there, and they let him go.
Yes, in school, usually.
But not so much.
I didn't experience so much, because I had a lot of friends, gentiles.
And we were a very big family in our town.
Our family consisted-- my grandfather had 85 children, grandchildren, and great-grandchildren.
And he was the first [NON-ENGLISH], that means statesman in the town.
And everybody looked up to him.
When I went in in a store-- I went in once in an [NON-ENGLISH].
Here, it is a drug store.
So the owner said to the other fellow, do you know who she is?
She's Koper's granddaughter.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were very-- we had two-- my mother and three sisters lived there.
And one brother lived in [INAUDIBLE], one in Radom, and one in Warsaw.
And my grandfather remarried and had three children with the second wife.
It was a [NON-ENGLISH] Paula, and [NON-ENGLISH], the youngest.
He worked for the SS.
They liked him very much.
But anyway, they sent him to Treblinka.
Well, we used to see a lot of people coming, walking through the town.
Whoever had a big apartment took them in.
But we were six children, and my sister was a dress maker, a seamstress.
And we couldn't-- didn't have anywhere to put them in two rooms.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We slept two in one bed.
In a small bed.
And we had to put up another bed for the night.
I was, that time, in Warsaw.
I went through everything from Warsaw.
Yes.
We was staying then-- my sister came home from the hospital.
She gave birth five days before.
And she-- we had to take her home.
And we ran back and forth to the basement and up for the little baby.
And it was very, very bad.
But, like I said, I was a tomboy.
So I stayed in lines.
And one time I brought five breads.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When you stayed in the line, when somebody in the front recognized you, and the back recognized you, you could go away for awhile.
So when I went out for my line, I went to the front.
And I pushed myself in.
And we went in in the courtyard, where the bakery is, and they gave you a bread.
I didn't go out from that courtyard.
I put myself in the line again in there.
I got already two breads.
I went-- I ran out, and I went home, fast.
It wasn't far.
And I came back to my line, and the people recognized me, so I went again like this.
So I brought home six breads.
This was like a million dollars now.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And after, when they came to Warsaw, the Germans, my sister came from-- they thought we were all dead-- from Bialobrzegi.
She came to Warsaw, walking and hitchhiking.
And she found us.
And that time, we went home to our town with the baby and my sister.
And my brother-in-law stayed in Warsaw because he had to work.
He had a factory.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that-- that was when they came in.
But later, it was a ghetto.
My father had to come home, because he usually was in the woods always.
For the summer I used to go stay with him.
He just came home for Saturday.
So he started to teach children Hebrew, [NON-ENGLISH].
And I started to bake bread.
I was kneading maybe, 50 pounds of flour.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I wasn't afraid.
I used to go across the SS.
They didn't recognize me.
My sister, if she had to bring to somebody a bread, she was so scared that they could've recognized her right away, that she's hiding something.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So this went on for a long time-- for a long time.
And my other sister went back to Warsaw when they closed the Warsaw ghetto.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it started to be very bad in our town.
So one time, I hear-- when they put us in the ghetto, I hear a knock on the door.
I went to the door to open.
So right away, I got two whips.
Where's the man?
Where's the man?
The SS.
I said, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
I don't have a husband.
I have an old father.
He was 52.
He looked so old.
So he walked in.
He gave my father a few whips.
We had to get off the bed.
And I had fresh bread in the house.
It's-- I don't know, that was above us-- and soap, I was selling.
He looked under the mattresses.
And we were just me, my sister, my father, my mother.
He saw he looked so old.
He looked in other closets, but he didn't smell the bread.
And he didn't see nothing.
He walked out.
When I opened the door, I saw a boy with a shovel holding in the hand.
I recognized this boy was my age.
He went with them.
He got five pounds of sugar for burying somebody.
So he said-- the SS man walked out.
I said, can I close the door, in German.
He said, yeah.
He walked in the front of the house.
He went into another family with three young children.
And this guy was working for the community.
He was a [NON-ENGLISH], big shot.
He took him out and shot him right in front of the door.
And this other guy with the shovel buried him right under the window.
We didn't see it.
We saw it in the morning.
We heard shooting, but I didn't see it because I locked the door.
I couldn't get out.
And we had rucksacks.
You call it rucksack.
In the morning, we'd put them out in a-- used to call a [INAUDIBLE] someplace.
For the night, we took them in, because we expected any, any, any day should be the [NON-ENGLISH].
But we were afraid for our father.
When they caught a guy with a beard, they ripped pieces from him.
So we took him in a barber, and he shaved our father's beard.
When we saw him start crying-- so he said don't cry, my children.
If I have a chin, I have a beard.
This was-- it didn't take long.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
It was-- my brother came.
He had a policeman's hat, just to walk around when it's going to be necessary.
So he came.
He said, you should know that tomorrow is going to be the [NON-ENGLISH].
So what could we do?
It was like a schlachthaus.
You know what's a schlachthaus, when they kill cattles?
That's what was on the date of the [NON-ENGLISH].
They were shooting.
They didn't look-- young, old, anybody, anybody who was walking not so fast, everybody, they knocked on our window.
We should out.
We should go on the big market there-- big market.
We all went there, and they start shooting, shooting even there, collecting.
This was selecting people.
He gave me a piece of paper, my brother, a note that maybe I'll be safe.
We all stayed in one line.
My sister and my mother stayed in front of me in a line.
I stayed in the back of her with somebody-- my sister-in-law.
And my father was in back of us with the men.
Do you remember the date, or the time of year?
Yes.
This was in 1942.
October the 1st.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they start-- my brother was trying to take out us in that-- like, he was-- one side was for dead, and one side was for live.
So he started to take out me, my sister, my mother.
So the SS saw this.
They start beating him, whipping him.
He was laying on the floor, almost unconscious.
[NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
And he barely got up.
And he saw that they pointed out my mother should go out in that line.
And when my mother went, my sister didn't wait.
They should call her.
She went right away with her.
And I went in another line.
And my sister-in-law, my brother's wife, went with me in that line.
We didn't know if we're going to be alive or not.
And he went to say goodbye to our father.
So my father said, remember.
I leave just one child.
Be a father and a brother to her.
But it didn't take long.
He was working with the [NON-ENGLISH] in the town.
You know what's a [NON-ENGLISH].
And they were shot-- both of them-- nice, tall.
My father was very smart-- very smart man.
If they needed-- some people had some misunderstanding, they called my father.
He should straighten them out.
He was so good.
He was on a [? payroll. ?] Every Sunday, he lent some people, like, 30 zlotys, 40 zlotys.
And Wednesday, they brought it back without anything.
He did a lot of good stuff.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were 10 women. 10 women were staying.
And men, was a lot.
